text:
Good evening, friends. Very happy to be here tonight, again, serving the Lord. Trusting with all my heart for a great outpouring of His divine blessings tonight, on us all.
I just did get here. I was just coming in in the back, and they were waiting out there for me---one of the ushers, and my boy. Just then, I heard "Only Believe," and we just had to hurry to get in. And so we are sorry to be just a little late.
Brother Caubles was standing at the door and told me to remember tonight about praying over the handkerchiefs. And that's very fine. We like to do that, because we've had a great lot of blessings for the people. We send out many handkerchiefs each day around the world for sick and afflicted people, and have a great success in doing so. And I'm sure that it'll be a great blessing to all, even if you don't need it at the present time, to carry around your house some little symbol of that.
text_no:
God kveld, venner. Det er en glede å være her i kveld, igjen, for å tjene Herren. Jeg stoler med hele mitt hjerte på en stor utgytelse av Hans guddommelige velsignelser over oss alle i kveld.
Jeg har nettopp ankommet. Jeg kom inn bakveien, og en av vaktmestrene og min sønn ventet på meg der ute. Akkurat da hørte jeg "Only Believe," og vi måtte skynde oss inn. Vi beklager at vi er litt forsinket.
Bror Caubles sto ved døren og minnet meg på å be over lommetørklærne i kveld. Det er veldig fint. Vi liker å gjøre det, da vi har hatt mange velsignelser for folk. Vi sender ut mange lommetørklær daglig til syke og lidende mennesker over hele verden, og har stor suksess med det. Jeg er sikker på at det vil være en stor velsignelse for alle, selv om du ikke trenger det for øyeblikket. Det kan være fint å ha et lite symbol rundt huset ditt.

text:
Here some time ago, I was… I have a testimony that … now, this, I never seen this, so I wouldn't know. But I have no right to doubt it, because it was a minister, and I believe, a very good Christian man. And he had one of those handkerchiefs in his Bible, keeping it on Acts 19. And the usual … asked him to do it. And his boy was struck down by a car.
And, now they tell me that they'd taken him to the hospital, put him under x-rays; and the bones were so broken that they had to handle him lightly, and---his arms and legs---and his back was out of place. Now, this is his story. And they went and got the handkerchiefs and laid it on the boy. And the next morning, when they went up to x-ray to see how to set the bones, there wasn't a broken bone in his body. That's right. Now, I never seen it, I only know the man. That's the testimony.
text_no:
For en tid tilbake var det en hendelse som jeg ønsker å vitne om. Jeg har ikke selv sett dette skje, så jeg kan ikke bekrefte det med sikkerhet. Men jeg har ingen grunn til å tvile, for det var en forkynner, som jeg tror er en svært god kristen mann, som fortalte meg det.
Han hadde en av de lommetørklærne som ligger i Bibelen hans, oppbevart på Apostlenes gjerninger 19. Etter forespørsel om å bruke det, skjedde det en ulykke der sønnen hans ble påkjørt av en bil. Familien tok ham til sykehuset for røntgenundersøkelse, og legene fant at beinene hans var så brutte at de måtte behandle ham svært forsiktig. Armen, bena og ryggen hans var ute av stilling.
Ifølge hans fortelling tok de frem lommetørkleet og la det på gutten. Neste morgen, da de skulle ta nye røntgenbilder for å se hvordan de kunne sette beinene, oppdaget de at gutten ikke hadde ett eneste brukket bein. Dette er altså hans vitnesbyrd, og mens jeg ikke har sett det selv, kjenner jeg mannen godt.

text:
I know this testimony: Some time ago (It just seemed to be so remarkable to me.), there was a… It happened in, I believe it was Fayetteville, Arkansas, or near there; I was having services. And I'd ask the people with handkerchiefs, I'd pray over them and have them put them in their Bible.
And so, I was at Jonesboro at the time. And a little lady that had her handkerchief prayed over, and she put it in the Bible. So they… One day, about two or three weeks later, she was washing … or, cleaning, a lamp chimney.
Did any of you ever clean a lamp chimney? I used to have to do it at home, 'cause I had the smallest hand. My mother's sitting there looking at me; she remembers that. We used to have a big old lamp chimney that had a moon and owl on it. Do you remember them old chimneys? I'd get my hand up in there and clean the chimney out. Did you ever run out of coal oil, lamp oil, kerosene, or what you call it? and had to put some water in the bottom of the thing to make the wick reach the kerosene? Did you ever do that to make it burn?
Say, I'm right at home tonight amongst country folks, sure enough. So we just live here in the city, we're still country folks, aren't we?
text_no:
Jeg kjenner til dette vitnesbyrdet: For en tid siden - det virket så bemerkelsesverdig for meg - skjedde det noe i, jeg tror det var Fayetteville, Arkansas, eller i nærheten der. Jeg holdt møter og ba folk gi meg lommetørklær så jeg kunne be over dem for deretter å legge dem i Bibelen sin.
Jeg var i Jonesboro på den tiden. En liten dame hadde fått sitt lommetørkle bedt over, og hun la det i Bibelen sin. Så, en dag, omtrent to eller tre uker senere, holdt hun på å vaske, eller rense, en lampepipe.
Er det noen som noen gang har renset en lampepipe? Jeg måtte ofte gjøre det hjemme fordi jeg hadde den minste hånden. Min mor sitter der og ser på meg; hun husker det godt. Vi hadde en stor gammel lampepipe med en måne og ugle på. Husker dere de gamle lampene? Jeg fikk hånden oppi pipen og renset den. Har du noen gang gått tom for lampeolje eller parafin, og måtte fylle litt vann i bunnen av lampa for å få veken til å nå oljen? Har du noen gang gjort det for å få den til å brenne?
I kveld føler jeg meg hjemme blant dere landlige folk, virkelig. Selv om vi bor i byen, er vi fortsatt landsens folk, er vi ikke?

text:
And this young married woman, she was washing her lamp chimney out. And her husband had gone to the city to get food and food for his cattle, and so forth. They were cotton raisers. And she cut the artery, or the vein, whatever it was in her wrist there. And the blood started spurting up.
Well, the first thing she done was first the old superstition to get some cobwebs and put on it. Well, she went and got cobwebs; 'course the blood would just throw the cobwebs off. It wouldn't do nothing. It might bring some kind of a disease to it anyhow, 'cause it's just a superstition. And she bled… One of the pillow slips got real wet with blood---saturated. Then she got a sheet. And her closest neighbor's about two miles. You'd have to walk in with boots on if you didn't drive the team.
And so, she seen that she was getting weaker and weaker. She couldn't live much longer. And the blood wouldn't stop. She was trying to hold it, and screaming and frantic.
Now, this is her story. She went and thought of that handkerchief that was in the Bible, and went to the… I don't say the handkerchief done it, now. It was her faith that did it, see. She went and she said, "God, I'm dying and there's no help that I can get anywhere. But, if You'll just help me…", and went and got this handkerchief, and laid it on her hand, and never even stained the handkerchief.
text_no:
Denne unge, gifte kvinnen vasket lampeglasset. Hennes mann hadde reist til byen for å skaffe mat, både til familien og dyrene. De drev med bomullsproduksjon. Hun kuttet en arterie eller åre i håndleddet, og blodet begynte å sprute ut.
Som første tiltak fulgte hun en gammel overtro og fikk tak i noen spindelvev. Men blodet kastet bare av spindelvevet; det virket overhodet ikke og kunne dessuten ha ført til infeksjoner. Blodet fortsatte å fosse, og en av putetrekkene ble gjennombløtt. Hun tok deretter en laken. Nærmeste nabo bodde omtrent to mil unna, og man måtte gå i støvler om man ikke kjørte hest og vogn.
Hun merket at hun ble svakere og svakere. Blodet stanset ikke, og hun innså at hun ikke ville overleve mye lenger. I desperasjon forsøkte hun å holde igjen blodet og ropte frenetisk om hjelp.
Ifølge hennes egen fortelling tenkte hun på et lommetørkle hun hadde liggende i Bibelen. Jeg påstår ikke at det var selve lommetørkleet som virket, men hennes tro som gjorde det. Hun ba til Gud om hjelp idet hun visste at hun var døende og ikke kunne få hjelp noen andre steder. Hun tok lommetørkleet og la det på såret. Blodet stanset umiddelbart, og lommetørkleet ble ikke engang flekket av blodet.

text:
And that same night she walked with boots on out through a muddy field for two miles, or better, to a highway. And got out of her boots and had her shoes under her arm.
I've seen a many time, young ladies, sixteen and eighteen years old, very fair; coming to church packing their shoes under their arms (not fifty years ago---last year), and get near a church and sit down on the side of the road and dust off their feet and pull on their hosiery, put on their stockings, and come to church. Might be people in here did that a long time ago, but that still exists down in Arkansas and some of those countries.
Then she gave the testimony, come by Greyhound bus, hung her lantern on a limb where she seen how to get out to the bus. And came in and gave that testimony, walking in the building before thousands of people at Little Rock. And gave the testimony that the blood stopped immediately as soon as she put the handkerchief on.
text_no:
Samme natt gikk hun med støvler gjennom et gjørmete jorde i over tre kilometer. Deretter tok hun av seg støvlene og bar skoene under armen.
Jeg har ofte sett unge damer, seksten til atten år gamle, komme til menigheten med skoene under armen. De setter seg langs veikanten, børster av føttene, tar på seg strømper, og går inn i menigheten. Dette skjedde ikke for femti år siden, men i fjor. Kanskje noen her gjorde dette for lenge siden, men det skjer fortsatt i noen områder, som i Arkansas.
Hun ga sitt vitnesbyrd etter å ha reist med Greyhound-bussen og hengt lykten på en gren for å se veien til bussen. Hun kom inn i bygningen og forkynte vitnesbyrdet foran tusenvis av mennesker i Little Rock. Hun fortalte at blødningen stoppet umiddelbart da hun la lommetørkleet på.

text:
I'm remembering another one just while waiting and bringing things just… One time there was a little lady, in first part of the ministry. It was a great upset that night, It just lives old memories over. A lady was standing with a little baby, very typical southern type, with his little legs around her hips, like this. And she was holding it. And the woman had a female trouble. And I prayed for her, and it just looked black around her, and I prayed again. This has been about, I guess, eight years ago. And I prayed again, and it just … it stayed black right around her.
Well, in them days, sometimes I just stayed with a patient till I had some assurance of something. And I prayed again. And I noticed… Very seldom ever a vision taken place. I'd know what was wrong by just a vision.
And there was the lady. And a horrible thing had happened. And it told her of living untrue to her marriage vow, which just a few days before that. She started crying, dropped her head down. And the man behind her was her husband. He pulled her around and said, "What's this?"
And she started confessing.
And it turned around and told him of the same thing for him.
And they put their arms around one another, pulled the little baby between them, and forgave one another. And knelt down there and gave their hearts to Christ. And the minister baptized them in the church the next morning.
text_no:
Jeg husker en annen hendelse mens jeg venter og bringer ting tilbake... En gang var det en liten dame tidlig i tjenesten min. Det var en stor oppstandelse den kvelden, og det vekker gamle minner. En kvinne stod med en liten baby, en typisk sørlig type, med bena rundt hoftene hennes. Hun holdt fast på barnet. Kvinnen hadde en kvinnesykdom, og jeg ba for henne, men det så bare svart ut rundt henne. Jeg ba igjen, dette er omtrent åtte år siden, og det forble svart rundt henne.
På den tiden pleide jeg av og til å bli hos en pasient til jeg hadde en viss forsikring om noe. Jeg ba igjen. Veldig sjelden skjedde det at en visjon fant sted, men jeg visste hva som var galt gjennom en visjon.
Denne kvinnen hadde opplevd noe fryktelig. Visjonen avslørte at hun hadde vært utro mot sine ekteskapsløfter bare noen få dager tidligere. Hun begynte å gråte og senket hodet. Mannen bak henne, som var hennes ektemann, trakk henne rundt og sa: "Hva er dette?"
Hun begynte å bekjenne.
Så vendte det seg mot ham og avslørte det samme for ham.
De la armene rundt hverandre, trakk barnet mellom seg og tilga hverandre. De knelte ned der og ga sine hjerter til Kristus. Og forkynneren døpte dem i menigheten neste morgen.

text:
That night coming through, there was a lady wanted a handkerchief prayed over like you're coming tonight. It was sent to a club-footed baby, way down somewhere in the country. And so, the lady, not being at the service, not knowing instructions, not knowing how to take a hold of faith… That's a hard thing, then.
So they just… The lady put the handkerchief… The lady told her, said, "Tie it around it's ankle."
Well, the handkerchief stayed on there for nearly three months. It had gotten dirty, and she'd washed it. And she wrote and told her sister that sent it, said, "There must not be nothing to that."
Said, "Well, if your faith ain't strong enough for it, mine is." She said, "You just keep it on there."
Said, "Well, the thing's just coming to pieces."
She said, "Tie strings on it, and tie it around anyhow. Keep it right there."
For regardless of what taken place, how long, you're the one that gets in a hurry, not God.
And one morning, not many mornings after that, they went back to the crib and found the baby normal and well.
text_no:
Den kvelden var det en dame som ønsket å få bedt over et lommetørkle, akkurat som du tenker på i kveld. Det ble sendt til en baby med klumpfot, langt ute på landet. Damen som mottok lommetørkleet, var ikke tilstede på møtet og kjente ikke til instruksjonene eller hvordan hun skulle gripe troen… Det kan være vanskelig.
Så de... Damen bandt lommetørkleet rundt ankelen på babyen.
Lommetørkleet ble sittende der i nesten tre måneder og ble skittent, så hun vasket det. Hun skrev til søsteren som hadde sendt det, og sa: "Det må ikke være noe kraft i dette."
Søsteren svarte: "Vel, hvis din tro ikke er sterk nok, så er min det. Bare hold det der."
Hun sa: "Men det begynner å gå i oppløsning."
Søsteren svarte: "Bind tråder rundt det og hold det på plass. Bare hold det der."
For uansett hvor lang tid det tar, er det vi som blir utålmodige, ikke Gud.
Og en morgen, ikke lenge etter, gikk de tilbake til barnesengen og fant babyen normal og frisk.

text:
Now, many things like that has happened … handkerchiefs. Now it is… It's not some… It's Scripture. If you think it's superstitious to believe the Scripture, then it would be a superstition.
But, now, many people anoint the handkerchiefs, anoint them in oil. Well, that's perfectly all right. Whatever our Lord will bless, I'm sure for it. I know you are too. But, if you'll watch the Scripture, they never anointed the handkerchiefs. They taken from the body of Paul handkerchiefs, or aprons---the body of Paul---handkerchiefs, and aprons, and little things that he wore on him.
I often wondered where they got that to be Scripture. And I believe they got it here (I'm not sure where Paul picked it up.) when the Shunammite woman went to Elisha, and he told her he said … told Gehazi, said, "Take my staff and go lay it on the baby."
Now, if the Lord hadn't just put something on my heart a few minutes ago standing there, I'd speak on this tonight. But tonight's Friday night, and I know I keep you late; and tomorrow's a big rushing day. I'll let you out early tonight, and hold you a long time tomorrow night.
text_no:
Nå, mange slike ting har skjedd ... lommetørklær. Men det er Skriften. Hvis du tror det er overtroisk å tro på Skriften, så ville det vært overtro.
Men, mange mennesker salver lommetørklær med olje. Det er helt i orden. Alt vår Herre vil velsigne, er jeg sikker på. Jeg vet at du er enig. Men, hvis du ser på Skriften, de salvet aldri lommetørklærne. De tok fra Paulus' kropp lommetørklær eller forklær — fra kroppen til Paulus — lommetørklær og små ting som han hadde på seg.
Jeg har ofte lurt på hvor de fikk det fra Skriften. Og jeg tror de fikk det herfra (jeg er ikke sikker på hvor Paulus plukket det opp) når den sunamittiske kvinnen gikk til Elisja, og han sa til Gehasi: "Ta min stav og legg den på barnet."
Hvis ikke Herren nettopp hadde lagt noe på mitt hjerte for noen minutter siden mens jeg stod der, ville jeg ha snakket om dette i kveld. Men i kveld er det fredag kveld, og jeg vet at jeg holder dere sent oppe; og morgendagen blir hektisk. Jeg slipper dere tidlig i kveld, og holder dere lenge i morgen kveld.

text:
So then, they…
The Shunammite woman went to Elisha, and Elisha took this staff, sent it by Gehazi, and said, "Lay it upon the child."
Now, Elisha knew that whatever he touched, when he had the anointing of the Spirit on him, that it was blessed. And I think that's what Paul did, and that's what the people saw. It wasn't Paul, it was God on Paul, and just a token, or a symbol of faith, you see. And so, they'd taken from his body handkerchiefs and aprons, and diseases went from the people.
What is that? What would you imagine? The people … some people touched the garment of Jesus and was made well. Others smacked Him in the face, and hit Him on the head with sticks, and spit in His face---never felt any virtue at all. It was their attitude of approach. It just depends on what attitude you come in. If you come believing that you're coming to get help, and you're confident that you're coming to the right person---Jesus Christ---and you're coming His provided way of approach, you're going to receive something, if you come reverently. If you come, "Well, I'll go touch and see if it helps me," that won't do you any good at all, see. It's your approach to God's divine article is what does it.
Just like the brass serpent: Why, the serpent didn't have no virtue in it; it was a piece of brass. They brought it up out of Egypt. Neither did the pole have any virtue in it---not a bit---but it was obeying what God said do. What brought the power, what brought the virtue is obedience to the Word of God, and that's the same thing tonight.
text_no:
Så, de...
Kvinnen fra Sunem gikk til Elisja, som tok staven sin, sendte den med Gehasi og sa: "Legg den på barnet."
Elisja visste at alt han berørte mens han var salvet av Ånden, var velsignet. Jeg tror det samme gjaldt Paulus, og det var dette folket så. Det var ikke Paulus selv, men Gud på Paulus, som et symbol på tro. De tok lommetørklær og forklær fra hans kropp, og sykdommer forlot folket.
Hva er det? Hva tror du? Noen mennesker rørte ved Jesu kledning og ble helbredet. Andre slo Ham i ansiktet, slo Ham på hodet med stokker og spyttet Ham i ansiktet - uten å merke noen kraft. Det handlet om deres måte å nærme seg Ham på. Hvis man tror man vil få hjelp og er trygg på at man kommer til den rette personen - Jesus Kristus - og nærmer seg på den foreskrevne måten, vil man motta noe dersom man kommer ærbødig. Hvis man derimot tenker: "Vel, jeg får prøve og se om det hjelper meg," vil det ikke gjøre noen nytte i det hele tatt. Det er måten man nærmer seg Guds hellige objekt som betyr noe.
Akkurat som kobberslangen: Slangen i seg selv hadde ingen kraft; det var et stykke kobber de hadde tatt med fra Egypt. Verken stangen den hang på hadde noen kraft, men det var lydigheten mot Guds Ord som brakte kraften. Og det samme gjelder i kveld.

text:
And now, as I pray over these handkerchiefs with all my heart, I would… If you'll excuse me for not doing it. Dr. Caubles, I'll ask you to help. Now, if you fail to get this, or if you're from any part of the country, you'll know your handkerchief. I don't know whether my secretary's here tonight or not. I suppose so, somewhere in the building. We send out scores of those things. Thousands a year goes all over the world; great testimonies returned. Now we'll be glad … it's free of charge. Everything we have is free.
Now, if you fail to get yours… We don't have handkerchiefs, 'cause it cost us so much---maybe twenty-five cents apiece. And you send out a couple thousand a week, you know what it would cost you. So we just don't have the money, but we buy hundreds of yards of ribbon. And I pray over that ribbon---cut it in little bitty pieces---and send it to all different parts of the world. And the people pin it on their underneath garment. Instructions is given on what to do. And we'll be glad to send…
Now, it's not that we want you to write to us, friends. I know a lot of these radio programs today; they: "Just write me. I want your address. Just let us know you're listening at us."
I have nothing against that, but you know yourself it's a publicity stunt to try to get your address to keep carding you and mailing you and billing you to send them money, and so forth. Which, that's all right. I have nothing against it. If God tells you to do that, all right. But, brother, we can hardly answer half the letters we get now, see. So, it isn't to get your address; it's trying to help you. That's the only alternative we have.
text_no:
Og nå, mens jeg ber over disse lommetørklene med hele mitt hjerte, vil jeg … Hvis dere unnskylder meg for ikke å fullføre dette selv, vil jeg be Dr. Caubles om hjelp. Hvis du ikke mottar dette, eller hvis du er fra en annen del av landet, vil du kjenne igjen ditt lommetørkle. Jeg vet ikke om sekretæren min er her i kveld eller ikke. Jeg antar det, et sted i bygningen. Vi sender ut mange av disse. Tusenvis årlig går over hele verden; store vitnesbyrd kommer tilbake. Vi sender dem gjerne … det er gratis. Alt vi har er gratis.
Hvis du ikke mottar ditt lommetørkle … Vi har ikke lommetørkler, fordi det koster oss mye—kanskje tjuefem øre per stykk. Og når du sender ut et par tusen i uken, vet du hva det vil koste. Så vi har ikke penger til det, men vi kjøper hundrevis av meter med bånd. Jeg ber over dette båndet—klipper det i små biter—og sender det til forskjellige deler av verden. Folk fester det på deres underplagg. Instruksjoner er gitt om hva de skal gjøre. Og vi sender det gjerne...
Det er ikke fordi vi ønsker at dere skal skrive til oss, venner. Jeg vet at mange av disse radioprogrammene i dag: "Bare skriv til meg. Jeg vil ha adressen din. La oss vite at du lytter til oss."
Jeg har ingenting imot det, men dere vet selv at det er et reklamegrep for å få adressen deres for kontinuerlig å sende dere kort, e-poster og regninger for å be om penger. Og det er greit, jeg har ingenting imot det. Hvis Gud har fortalt deg å gjøre det, så er det all right. Men, bror, vi klarer knapt å svare på halvparten av brevene vi får nå. Så, det er ikke for å få adressen din; det er for å hjelpe deg. Det er det eneste alternativet vi har.

text:
And now, I will pray for these after I'm sure His anointing Spirit is on me, see. It would be better, because I…
God, to serve Him is like a dual personality, I guess, or something. You go out here, you like to work and build, help to build a house, go fishing, or hunting, or whatever it is; you're just on your own then. But when that Spirit of God strikes you, it's something different then. It's the anointing of God using that individual vessel. And if it's truth … if it is the truth, then them words that come forth, uttered by a being---it's either God or the devil. You'd know that.
Now, it's what you think it is is what'll determine what you get from it, see. Now, if you believe it to be God, there's only two things could speak. It had to be supernatural, and in supernatural it only come from two resources: that's either God or the devil.
And I want somebody to ever put their finger on a scripture, or any person, that was ever healed by the devil, or any scripture that even pertained to the devil healing. The devil could put curses on you, but he can't take them off. The Lord's the only one who can heal sicknesses or diseases---is the Lord.
So, that's what they claim: Jesus, He was Beelzebub. They said He was Beelzebub because He could read and know what was wrong with those people; know what their diseases was, know what their names was, and who they was and where they come from. Not everybody, I suppose; He said just as the Father would show Him.
But they said, "Well, He's a fortuneteller." But the thing of it was, He healed the sick by that, and they couldn't understand that. So that got them all tore up. So they didn't know what to do about it. So it's the same thing tonight. But on this here, is God. Obeying His command is what God has said do.
text_no:
Nå vil jeg be for disse etter at jeg er sikker på at Hans salvede Ånd er over meg, ser du. Det ville vært bedre, fordi jeg ...
Å tjene Gud er som å ha en dobbel personlighet, tror jeg. Du går ut, liker å jobbe og bygge, hjelpe til med å bygge et hus, dra på fiske eller jakt eller hva det måtte være; da er du bare deg selv. Men når Guds Ånd kommer over deg, skjer det noe annerledes. Det er Guds salvelse som bruker dette individuelle kar. Og hvis det er sannhet ... hvis det er sannhet, så er de ordene som blir uttalt av et vesen enten fra Gud eller djevelen. Du ville visst det.
Nå, hva du tror det er, vil avgjøre hva du får ut av det, ser du. Hvis du tror det er fra Gud, er det bare to kilder det kan komme fra: enten Gud eller djevelen.
Og jeg utfordrer noen til å peke på en skrift, eller en person, som noen gang ble helbredet av djevelen, eller enhver skrift som til og med antydet at djevelen kunne helbrede. Djevelen kan legge forbannelser på deg, men han kan ikke fjerne dem. Herren er den eneste som kan helbrede sykdommer eller plager.
Så, det de påstod var at Jesus var Beelzebub. De sa Han var Beelzebub fordi Han kunne lese og vite hva som var galt med mennesker, kjenne deres sykdommer, navn, hvem de var og hvor de kom fra. Ikke alle, antar jeg; Han sa bare når Faderen viste Ham.
Men de sa: "Vel, Han er en spåmann." Men Han helbredet de syke med det, og de kunne ikke forstå det. Så det forvirret dem. De visste ikke hva de skulle gjøre med det. Det samme gjelder i kveld. Men her er det Gud. Å adlyde Hans bud er hva Gud har sagt.

text:
I am glad tonight. I don't know whether Brother Daugherty has raised up, or give a testimony, and Sister Daugherty. Is Betty with you, Brother Daugherty? One of my first books I published called, "I Was Not Disobedient To The Heavenly Vision"; to a little lady, a little girl, dying with something like St. Vitas dance, just screaming and going on. The doctors had give her up, and ministers had prayed.
I was having a meeting. There's men sitting right here tonight, pitched in fifty-cents apiece to pay my railroad fare to come over here in Louisville, catch a train to go to St. Louis to see this little girl.
And laying there in the bed. Poor Brother Daugherty, Sister Daugherty, all worn and torn. I was in there several hours, and went down to his church and prayed. And the poor little child laid there, gnawing its tongue, and eating a… Oh, horrible condition. A little blond-headed girl. Just very pathetic, like a animal, just screaming and going on. Screamed so much, and been sick so many months until it just didn't look like a human being.
And I waited, I went down to his church, I prayed, I made all kind of promises to God. Come back, and was sitting in his … a Ford car, sitting out in front of the house. I can just almost see it now. Looking out across the hood of the car, and there come a vision, "Thus saith the Lord."
That settled it. Where are you at, Betty honey. There she is, right there. All right. There's the little girl now, a young woman fine and healthy by God's grace. Her mother and dad sits there too, and her little sister.
text_no:
Jeg er glad i kveld. Jeg vet ikke om Bror Daugherty har stått opp eller gitt et vitnesbyrd, og det samme gjelder Søster Daugherty. Er Betty med deg, Bror Daugherty? En av mine første bøker, "Jeg Var Ikke Ulydig Mot Den Himmelske Visjonen", handler om en liten jente som holdt på å dø av noe som lignet St. Vitus-dans, bare skrikende og urolig. Legene hadde gitt henne opp, og predikanter hadde bedt for henne.
Jeg holdt et møte, og det er menn som sitter her i kveld som bidro med femti øre hver for å betale togbilletten min for å komme til Louisville og ta toget til St. Louis for å se denne lille jenta. Hun lå der i sengen, og stakkars Bror og Søster Daugherty var helt utslitte. Jeg var der i flere timer og gikk ned til menigheten deres for å be. Den stakkars lille jenta lå der, tygget på tungen og var i en forferdelig tilstand. En liten blond jente, veldig patetisk, som en dyrekropp bare skrikende og urolig. Hun hadde skreket og vært syk så lenge at hun ikke så ut som et menneske lenger.
Jeg ventet, gikk ned til deres menighet og ba, og lovet Gud mange ting. Jeg kom tilbake og satt i en Ford-bil foran huset deres. Jeg kan nesten se det for meg som det var i går. Mens jeg så ut over panseret på bilen, kom en visjon: "Så sier Herren."
Det avgjorde saken. Hvor er du, kjære Betty? Der er hun, rett der. Nå er hun en ung kvinne, sunn og frisk ved Guds nåde. Moren og faren hennes sitter også her, sammen med hennes lille søster.

text:
The first case that was my going to St. Louis, my call to there, went there for my first revival, begin with Betty's testimony in St. Louis, Missouri.
How many ever read that little book called "Heavenly Vision"? Amen. You remember testimony of the Daugherty family? That's the little lady sitting right there now.
How the Holy Spirit told me, said, to have Reverend … the young Reverend here, to stand to one side, and the father to stand to the other side, have the mother to get a little piece of white cloth and wet it, and stand there and be wiping down the baby's face and hands and to its feet, just as I repeated: "Our Father who art in heaven, hallowed be Thy name."
When it come right to the end then, the Spirit of the Lord come down, said just exactly what to do. There's Betty sitting there, testimony, living and well.
Well, He's just God, that's all. We just love Him. We're trying to think it's way off somewhere else, when it's right with us. "I'll be with you, even in you, to the end of the world."
text_no:
Det første tilfellet var da jeg dro til St. Louis etter å ha blitt kalt dit. Jeg dro dit for min første vekkelse, som begynte med Bettys vitnesbyrd i St. Louis, Missouri.
Hvor mange har noensinne lest den lille boken som heter "Heavenly Vision"? Amen. Husker dere vitnesbyrdet om Daugherty-familien? Det er den lille damen som sitter der borte nå.
Hvordan Den Hellige Ånd fortalte meg at den unge Reverenden skulle stå til den ene siden, og faren til den andre siden, mens moren skulle få en liten bit hvitt stoff, fukte det, og stå der og tørke babyens ansikt, hender og føtter, akkurat som jeg gjentok: "Vår Far som er i himmelen, helliget vorde Ditt navn."
Da vi kom til slutten, kom Herrens Ånd ned og fortalte nøyaktig hva vi skulle gjøre. Der sitter Betty, vitnesbyrdet levende og frisk.
Vel, Han er bare Gud, det er alt. Vi elsker Ham bare. Vi prøver å tenke at Han er langt borte, når Han er rett med oss. "Jeg vil være med dere, til og med i dere, til verdens ende."

text:
The Lord bless you now. And I promised that I'd try to let you out just as early as possible. I'll read a scripture here, and go right straight to the service, speak a little, and then we'll have the prayer for the sick.
Now, I think, if I'm not mistaken, Brother Caubles, is it Sunday night at the…? [Brother Caubles says, "Memorial Auditorium."] Memorial Auditorium. And so, that's down on Fourth Street---some of the visitors, and so forth. You've been real lovely this week, all of you, to come to the church. Sometimes people don't want to go to church. They say, "Well, these people, that's in a church…"
But remember, you're just as much interdenominational here as you would be in the auditorium, just the same. "No creed but Christ, no law but love, no book but the Bible." That's the way we do it. And we don't have… "We have fellowship one with the other, while the blood of Jesus Christ, God's Son, cleanses us from all unrighteousness." Isn't that wonderful?
text_no:
Herren velsigne deg nå. Jeg lovet at jeg skulle prøve å avslutte så tidlig som mulig. Jeg vil lese et skriftsted her, gå rett inn i møtet, tale litt, og deretter ha bønnen for de syke.
Nå, hvis jeg ikke tar feil, Bror Caubles, er det søndag kveld i…? [Bror Caubles sier: "Memorial Auditorium."] Memorial Auditorium. Så, det er nede på Fourth Street—til noen av besøkende, og så videre. Dere har vært svært hyggelige denne uken, alle sammen, ved å komme til menigheten. Noen ganger vil folk ikke gå til menighet. De sier: "Vel, de menneskene som er i en menighet…"
Men husk, dere er like mye interkonfesjonelle her som dere ville vært i auditoriet, akkurat det samme. "Ingen trosbekjennelse, bare Kristus; ingen lov, bare kjærlighet; ingen bok, bare Bibelen." Slik gjør vi det. Og vi har ikke… "Vi har fellesskap med hverandre, mens Jesu Kristi blod, Guds Sønn, renser oss fra all urettferdighet." Er ikke det vidunderlig?

text:
Now, in the book of St. Luke, and beginning with the 10th chapter and the 46th verse, I shall read just a portion of the Scripture.
And they came to Jericho: and as he went out of Jericho with his disciples and a great multitude of people, blind Bartimaeus, the son of Timaeus, sat by the highway side begging.
And when he heard that it was Jesus of Nazareth, he began to cry out, saying, Jesus, thou son of David, have mercy on me.
And many charged him that he should hold his peace: but he cried the more a great deal, Thou son of David, have mercy on me.
… Jesus stood still, and commanded him to be called. And they call the blind man, saying unto him, Be of good comfort, rise; he calleth thee.
And he, casting away his garment, rose, and came to Jesus.
And Jesus answered and said unto him, What wilt thou that I should do unto thee? The blind man said unto him, Lord, that I may receive my sight.
… Jesus said unto him, Go thy way; thy faith hath made thee whole … immediately he received his sight, and followed Jesus in the way.
text_no:
I boken til Lukas, kapittel 10, vers 46, vil jeg lese en del av Skriften:
"De kom til Jeriko. Da Jesus dro ut fra Jeriko med sine disipler og en stor folkemengde, satt den blinde Bartimeus, sønn av Timeus, ved veikanten og tigget. Da han hørte at det var Jesus fra Nasaret, begynte han å rope: 'Jesus, Du Davids sønn, ha miskunn med meg!' Mange ba ham tie, men han ropte enda høyere: 'Du Davids sønn, ha miskunn med meg!'
Jesus stanset og sa at han skulle kalles til Ham. De kalte på den blinde og sa: 'Vær ved godt mot, reis deg; Han kaller på deg.' Han kastet av seg kappen, reiste seg og kom til Jesus. Jesus spurte ham: 'Hva vil du at Jeg skal gjøre for deg?' Den blinde svarte: 'Herre, la meg få synet mitt.' 
Jesus sa til ham: 'Gå din vei; din tro har frelst deg.' Straks fikk han synet tilbake, og han fulgte Jesus på veien."

text:
Shall we speak to this One now, that this is written after.
Kind Heavenly Father, we address Thee in Jesus' name, thanking Thee for this scripture, and for being alive today. Many things has changed in the world. Times has changed, transportation's changed, people has changed, but Thy Word never changes. It's forever the same. And I thank Thee for it.
And may that same lovely Jesus, and the faith of this blind man who could stop the Master on His road, may it stop Him tonight. Many of the people here that are sick, and may He heal them all. Forgive us of our sins and shortcomings. And may Thy love be with us tonight. And may it be brought through Your servant, as we being units, or offsprings of God, sons of God, a part of God, with God's Spirit to minister one to another.
And may the love of God so wrap our hearts together, that there'll be no place at all for doubts. And may just perfect love and faith exist in this building tonight. And may many be healed, saved, backsliders reclaimed, and the angels of heaven rejoice when the meeting's finished. For we ask it in Jesus Christ's name. Amen.
text_no:
La oss nå henvende oss til Denne som teksten handler om.
Kjære Himmelske Far, vi kommer til Deg i Jesu navn, og takker Deg for denne skriften og for at vi får være i live i dag. Mange ting har endret seg i verden. Tider har endret seg, transport har endret seg, mennesker har endret seg, men Ditt Ord endrer seg aldri. Det forblir alltid det samme. Og jeg takker Deg for det.
Måtte den samme kjære Jesus, og troen til denne blinde mannen som kunne stoppe Mesteren på Hans vei, stoppe Ham i kveld. Mange av menneskene her er syke; måtte Han helbrede dem alle. Tilgi oss våre synder og mangler. Måtte Din kjærlighet være med oss i kveld, og bringes gjennom Din tjener, mens vi, som enheter eller avkom fra Gud, Guds sønner, en del av Gud, med Guds Ånd, tjener hverandre.
Må Guds kjærlighet omslutte våre hjerter så fullstendig at det ikke finnes noe sted for tvil. Måtte bare perfekt kjærlighet og tro eksistere i dette bygget i kveld. Måtte mange bli helbredet, frelst, frafalne gjenopprettet, og himmelens engler glede seg når møtet er ferdig. For vi ber om dette i Jesu Kristi navn. Amen.

text:
Now, our scene for the next few moments is a tragic one in one way. There's hardly anything … way I could describe the darkness of this scene. It's a blind man; he's sitting by the walls of a great, wicked city.
And I've often thought that how that men could say that people were blind for the glory of God. I never could see that: a person shut off in darkness and blackness for the glory of God. God created man to have five senses, and then take the sense of sight away from him and say it's God's will. That's always been a hard thing for me to see. I can hardly believe it.
And just like I was talking to a fellow not long ago about … nothing about… If there, perhaps, may be a Catholic friend sitting here, I'm not saying this for any down. Remember, the Protestant religion needs to be built up a whole lot before the Catholics can be criticized. That's right. But the Carmelite order of the Catholic Church, them ladies to go in, young ladies, and just stay behind prison walls all their life. They'd be a lot better off, to my opinion, out trying to do something for the glory of God, I think.
But now, we Protestants have things just as bad. We know to do good and do it not, and then to us, it would be evil. But, some of those things are hard things for me to see. Maybe it's because of me. I don't know.
text_no:
Nå skal vi ta for oss en tragisk scene. Det er vanskelig å beskrive hvor mørk denne scenen er. En blind mann sitter ved murene til en stor, ond by.
Jeg har ofte tenkt på hvordan noen kan si at mennesker er blinde for Guds herlighet. Jeg har aldri forstått det: at en person holdes i mørke for Guds herlighet. Gud skapte mennesket med fem sanser, og så tar Han synet fra dem og sier at det er Hans vilje. Dette har alltid vært vanskelig for meg å akseptere. Jeg har vanskelig for å tro det.
Jeg snakket med en mann for ikke lenge siden om noe annet… Hvis det kanskje er en katolsk venn her, sier jeg ikke dette for å kritisere. Husk, den protestantiske religionen har mye å forbedre før vi kan kritisere katolikkene. Det er sant. Men den karmelittiske ordenen innen Den katolske kirke, der unge kvinner går inn og tilbringer hele livet bak murene… Etter min mening ville de ha vært bedre tjent med å gjøre noe for Guds herlighet.
Men vi protestanter har også våre problemer. Vi vet hva som er godt, men gjør det ikke, og for oss er det en synd. Noen av disse tingene er vanskelige for meg å forstå. Kanskje er det bare meg. Jeg vet ikke.

text:
But, this poor, blind man sitting outside of a big, wicked city; always was a wicked city. As far as I know, still a wicked city. In the Bible, it's represented as the roads to hell. Jerusalem sits on a hill; Jericho sits in the valley.
And the good Samaritan found the man coming from Jerusalem, down to Jericho when he fell among thieves. Think of it: He was backsliding---coming from the heavenly to the cursed. Jericho is a cursed city. Jerusalem is a blessed city. And he was on his road, backsliding, when he fell among thieves and robbers that stripped him.
And that's when you'll fall among thieves and robbers, when you start backsliding away from God. Just expect the enemy to be along the road.
text_no:
Denne stakkars, blinde mannen satt utenfor en stor, ond by; den har alltid vært en ond by. Så vidt jeg vet, er den fortsatt ond. I Bibelen er den representert som veien til helvete. Jerusalem ligger på en høyde; Jeriko ligger i en dal.
Den gode samaritanen fant mannen komme fra Jerusalem på vei ned til Jeriko, da han falt blant røvere. Tenk på det: Han var frafallen---kom fra det himmelske til det forbannede. Jeriko er en forbannet by. Jerusalem er en velsignet by. Mannen var på vei til å falle fra når han ble overfalt av røvere som ribbet ham.
Og det er da du vil falle blant tyver og røvere, når du begynner å falle fra Gud. Du kan forvente at fienden ligger langs veien.

text:
And here we see this blind Bartimaeus sitting at the gate, maybe a cold November day, and the wind's a-blowing. His ragged coat's … and he's sitting there. Not only that, but perhaps hungry---a man blind, and then hungry.
Many beggars was in that day, so he had a lot of competition. Very few nickels was given to him. I imagine there was so many beggars till there was no way at all the man could have any assurance. And everything that he had, he had to beg for it. And so many other beggars along; just now and then he got a coin.
And that was a hard life. There's no surgery. And if there was such a thing as surgery for his eyes, well, he didn't have the money to pay for it. And perhaps the doctors wouldn't have done it without money. I doubt them having surgery of that type, or maybe in his case. I understand, according to some theologians, that he was born blind.
So the man, anyhow, was blind, sitting there, winter time coming on: Hopeless, helpless, nothing to eat, hungry; sitting outside of the walls of a great, cruel city; and it coming wintertime, shivering in the cold. What a tragic scene. My! That could picture some darkness. And not knowing that just down the road a little piece come relief for him.
text_no:
Her ser vi den blinde Bartimeus sittende ved porten, kanskje en kald novemberdag, med vinden som blåser. Hans fillete kappe er … og han sitter der. Ikke bare det, men han er kanskje også sulten—en blind mann og i tillegg sulten.
Det var mange tiggere på den tiden, så han hadde mye konkurranse. Svært få mynter ble gitt til ham. Jeg kan forestille meg at det var så mange tiggere at mannen ikke kunne ha noen form for trygghet. Alt han hadde, måtte han tigge etter. Og med så mange andre tiggere rundt seg, fikk han bare av og til en mynt.
Det var et hardt liv. Det fantes ingen operasjon. Og selv om det hadde vært mulig med en operasjon for øynene hans, hadde han ikke penger til å betale for det. Legene ville kanskje ikke ha gjort det uten betaling. Jeg tviler på at slike operasjoner eksisterte, eller at det var mulig i hans tilfelle. Ifølge noen teologer, var han født blind.
Så mannen var uansett blind, sittende der, vinteren nærmet seg: Håpløs, hjelpeløs, ingenting å spise, sulten; sittende utenfor murene til en stor, grusom by; og vinteren kom, og han skalv i kulda. For en tragisk scene. Det kunne virkelig beskrive noe mørkt. Og uten å vite at hjelpen var like nede i veien for ham.

text:
There might be some of you sitting here tonight in almost in that condition: Hopeless as far as medical is concerned, helpless as far as any help is concerned, and don't know that maybe standing right near you, stands your healing, just as close as it was to blind Bartimaeus.
And there he was sitting at the gate, begging. And I can imagine as he was sitting there, begging, coins… It'd been perhaps, wintertime coming on; not many travelers that day. The great stones---cobblestones---in the road. That road led from Jericho to Jerusalem was well polished with travelers as they traveled over it.
The great Joshua, the conqueror for Israel, led the Israelites up this same road to Jericho. And also on that same road went Elijah and Elisha one day to the translation of Elijah. Many great historical marks was along the side of the road, but the scream had come like it is in Louisville, Kentucky, tonight: "The days of miracles is past."
Poor fellow, without a hope, sitting outside of the gates; no church to take him in, nobody to help him. And what help could be done couldn't console him, because the man was blind and he wanted his sight. And there he's sitting here.
text_no:
Noen av dere som sitter her i kveld, kan være i en lignende tilstand: Håpløs med tanke på medisinske muligheter, hjelpeløs uten noen å vende seg til, og uvitende om at din helbredelse kanskje står like i nærheten, like nær som den var for den blinde Bartimeus.
Der satt han ved porten og tigget. Jeg kan forestille meg at det var vintertid, og ikke mange reisende den dagen. De store brosteinene i veien, som ledet fra Jeriko til Jerusalem, var godt polert av de mange reisende som hadde gått der.
Den store Josva, Israels erobrer, ledet israelittene opp denne samme veien til Jeriko. Også profetene Elia og Elisja gikk denne veien da Elia ble tatt opp til himmelen. Mange store historiske hendelser fant sted langs denne veien, men ropet hadde spredt seg som i Louisville, Kentucky, i kveld: "Miraklenes tid er forbi."
Den stakkars mannen, uten håp, satt utenfor portene; ingen menighet for å ta ham inn, ingen som kunne hjelpe ham. Og det som kunne gjøres av hjelp, kunne ikke trøste ham, fordi mannen var blind og ønsket å få synet tilbake. Der sitter han.

text:
Then I can imagine, the first thing you know, somebody begin to slip by. He begged for a coin, sitting in his prison of darkness, and no coin dropped in. He hears something. Now, after a while he hears some footsteps approaching. What is it? Somebody says, "Jesus of Nazareth is passing by."
Oh, my. "Faith cometh by hearing." Somebody had told him that there was a man who prayed for the sick and opened the eyes of the blind---healed the sick. He was God's Son.
Now, but what little chance did blind Bartimaeus have, sitting by the side of the gate. A beggar himself, and in this distress, sitting there, and all hopes gone. And yet, the only hope that he had was passing by.
Now, he had one thing he could do: that was call.
text_no:
Jeg kan forestille meg at, før du vet ordet av det, begynner noen å snike seg forbi. Han ba om en mynt mens han satt i sitt mørke fengsel, men ingen mynt falt ned. Han hører noe. Etter en stund hører han fottrinn nærme seg. Hva er det? Noen sier: "Jesus fra Nasaret går forbi."
Å, min. "Tro kommer av å høre." Noen hadde fortalt ham at det fantes en mann som ba for de syke, åpnet øynene til de blinde og helbredet de syke. Han var Guds Sønn.
Men hvilke sjanser hadde blinde Bartimeus, sittende ved porten? En tigger i nød, sittende der, uten håp. Og likevel, det eneste håpet han hadde, var i ferd med å gå forbi. 
Han hadde én ting han kunne gjøre: rope.

text:
Now, if faith cometh by hearing, hearing of the Word, and he knew that Jesus come by, he couldn't say, "Now, wait. I'm might see Him another day. I'll try tomorrow night at the next meeting." He didn't have that opportunity. That was his first and last opportunity. The only opportunity he ever had, or ever would have, to ever receive his sight.
So, he had to do something. He raised, and screamed to the top of his voice, "Jesus, Thou Son of David, have mercy on me! Have mercy on me!"
There, Jesus facing up that road, a road that… You and I, tonight, all of us are guilty of sending Him up that road. On His mind He knew that He was going to Calvary up that road. On that road He knowed He was going to Gethsemane. He was going to Pilate's judgment hall. He was to be beaten, mocked, and His great gifts of God was to be put at trial. Going to put a rag around His head and smack Him on the head and say, "Now, if You know all what's in the people's hearts and minds, tell me who hit you."
All those things faced Him, a-quivering, bittering, death there He had to face. He knew also, hanging in His mind there, that He was going up to have His hands placed around a pole, His clothes stripped off of Him, and lashes that would burn plumb into the bone was going around Him. And His blood would bathe His side. And a cruel, thorn crown would be placed on His head. And He'd be nailed to a cross, and hang there, bleeding, bleating, dying, as the most bloody sacrifice was ever offered in the world. And all of that confronting Him. And He's walking; I can see Him in His air of quietness as He walked straight and the disciples going along, putting the people off the road.
text_no:
Nå, dersom tro kommer av å høre, å høre Ordet, og han visste at Jesus kom forbi, kunne han ikke si: "Vent litt. Jeg kan se Ham en annen dag. Jeg prøver igjen i morgen kveld på neste møte." Han hadde ikke den muligheten. Dette var hans første og siste sjanse, den eneste sjansen han noen gang ville ha, for å få sitt syn.
Så han måtte gjøre noe. Han reiste seg og skrek for full hals: "Jesus, Du Davids Sønn, miskunn Deg over meg! Miskunn Deg over meg!"
Der gikk Jesus opp den veien, en vei som... Du og jeg, i kveld, alle av oss er skyldige i å sende Ham opp den veien. I Sine tanker visste Han at Han skulle til Golgata opp den veien. På den veien visste Han at Han skulle til Getsemane. Han skulle til Pilatus' domshall. Han skulle bli slått, hånet, og Hans store gaver fra Gud skulle settes på prøve. De skulle binde et klede rundt Hans hode og slå Ham i hodet og si: "Nå, hvis Du vet alt hva som er i folks hjerter og sinn, fortell meg hvem som slo Deg."
Alle disse tingene ventet Ham, en skjelvende, bitter død Han måtte møte. Han visste også, hengende i tankene Sine, at Han skulle få hendene bundet rundt en stang, klærne revet av, og piskeslag som ville brenne inn til beinet skulle regne over Ham. Og Hans blod ville bade Hans side. Og en grusom tornekrone skulle plasseres på Hans hode. Og Han skulle bli spikret til et kors, og henge der, blødende, sårt brølende, døende, som det mest blodige offer som noen gang har blitt tilbudt i verden. Alt dette ventet Ham. Og Han gikk; Jeg kan se Ham i Sin stillhet mens Han gikk rett frem og disiplene gikk med, holdende folket unna veien.

text:
And to think what little chance with all of that hanging before Him, did this poor, unseen, unthought-of, blind beggar, hunched against the wall… When businessmen come by, half … one-half, not even a speck of authority to the size of this would pass him by without giving him a penny. But, yet, how is he going to attract that man? With one thing: "Thou Son of David, have mercy on me."
And to think, with all that hanging in front of Jesus, that blind beggar's cry stopped Him still in His tracks. He stopped.
Oh, friends, His love and compassion for the sick was so great, till no matter what the task was ahead of Him, He was always willing to minister to those who were in need. It stopped Him still, a cry from an unprivileged … from a blind beggar. An old, ragged beggar standing by the side of the wall stopped the Son of God still in His journey.
Your cry tonight will stop Him. He's passing through here tonight. Your cry will stop Him.
He said, "What…? Bring him here to Me."
And they brought… [Unclear words],
text_no:
Og tenk hvilken liten sjanse denne fattige, usynlige, ubetenkte, blinde tiggeren hadde, bøyd mot veggen... Når forretningsmenn gikk forbi, ville ikke engang en ørliten aktelse for noen som ham hindre dem i å gi en eneste krone. Men likevel, hvordan skulle han få den mannens oppmerksomhet? Kun med én ting: "Du Davids Sønn, miskunn Deg over meg."
Og tenk, med alt som lå foran Jesus, stoppet den blinde tiggerens rop Ham i Hans spor. Han stanset.
Å, venner, Hans kjærlighet og medfølelse for de syke var så stor at uansett hvilken oppgave som lå foran Ham, var Han alltid villig til å hjelpe de som trengte det. Det stoppet Ham, et rop fra en ubesiktiget... fra en blind tigger. En gammel, fillete tigger som sto ved siden av veggen, stoppet Guds Sønn på Hans reise.
Ditt rop i kveld vil stoppe Ham. Han er her i kveld. Ditt rop vil stoppe Ham.
Han sa: "Hva...? Bring ham hit til Meg."
Og de førte ham... [Uklare ord],

text:
now. I want you to notice another dramatic thing here. Wish we had time to get into it. Something come to my mind.
Now, notice, he didn't know how he was going to find Him. The only thing he knowed, he sat in a dark world he'd never seen. But there he raised up, throwed aside his old, ragged coat. If it'd been an hour before that, if he'd have lost that coat, or if he'd had to lay it down, he'd folded it neatly, and laid it to one side because he knowed he couldn't find it any more. He was blind, hardly anybody would help him. But now faith takes a hold. Faith springs on what? "I've attracted the attention of the Son of God, and He's bid me come to Him."
Every one of you here tonight's got the same bidding. "Whosoever will, let him come."
"I've attracted His attention. He's called me. Now, I won't need the coat any more as long as I obey Him. He would've never give me the invitation if He didn't mean to give me help."
And He'd never been wounded for your transgressions if He hadn't have meant to take them away. He'd have never been striped for your healing, unless He meant to take it away from you.
text_no:
Legg nå merke til en annen dramatisk detalj her. Jeg skulle ønske vi hadde tid til å gå dypere inn på dette. Noe kom til tankene mine.
Merk, han visste ikke hvordan han skulle finne Ham. Det eneste han visste, var at han satt i en mørk verden han aldri hadde sett. Men der reiste han seg opp, kastet bort den gamle, fillete kappen. Hvis det hadde vært en time tidligere, hvis han hadde mistet kappen, eller hvis han hadde måttet legge den ned, ville han ha brettet den nøye og lagt den til side fordi han visste at han ikke kunne finne den igjen. Han var blind, og nesten ingen ville hjelpe ham. Men nå grep troen tak. Troen sprang på hva? "Jeg har tiltrukket meg Guds Sønns oppmerksomhet, og Han har bedt meg komme til Ham."
Hver og en av dere her i kveld har fått den samme invitasjonen. "Enhver som vil, kan komme."
"Jeg har tiltrukket Hans oppmerksomhet. Han har kalt meg. Nå trenger jeg ikke kappen mer, så lenge jeg følger Ham. Han ville aldri ha gitt meg invitasjonen hvis Han ikke mente å gi meg hjelp."
Han ville aldri blitt såret for dine overtredelser hvis Han ikke mente å ta dem bort. Han ville aldri blitt slått for din helbredelse med mindre Han mente å ta sykdommen fra deg.

text:
You've got His attention now. What do you do?
Here's faith going in action. Throws aside the garment. "I won't need it anymore. I'm going to be a new creature from now on." He throwed aside his garment.
"How can you see?"
Out in his dark, black world where he couldn't see nothing, he spread forth his ragged sleeve like that, and his bony arms sticking through it. He made a way to find Jesus. No matter what condition he was in, he had His attention. He didn't know which way he was going. He knowed it come from that direction. So here he goes with his arms stretched out. His ragged sleeves hanging down, tears running from the place where the eyes should be, running to Him. Didn't know how he was going to find Him, but the Word of God said, "Come," and he was going.
There's the main thing. No matter how sick you are, how bad you are, how many trials you've had. When He said, "Whosoever will, let him come," He meant you. That's right.
"How am I going to get to it, Brother Branham? The doctor said I can't get well."
Throw it aside. Let's go. We've got His attention tonight. He's passing this way. He's been here every night this week. He's here now. He'll be anywhere where two or three are gathered in His name: "I'll be in their midst. Whatever they agree upon and ask, they shall receive it." We've got His attention now. He's moving through the building.
Now, raise your hands of faith to Him and say, "Lord, that I might receive my sight."
He said,
text_no:
Du har nå fått Hans oppmerksomhet. Hva gjør du?
Her går tro i aksjon. Kaster til side plagget. "Jeg trenger det ikke lenger. Jeg skal være en ny skapning fra nå av." Han kastet bort sitt plagg.
"Hvordan kan du se?"
I sin mørke, svarte verden hvor han ikke kunne se noe, strakte han frem sin fillete erme med de beinete armene som stakk ut. Han fant en vei til Jesus, uansett hvilken tilstand han var i, han hadde Hans oppmerksomhet. Han visste ikke hvilken vei han gikk, men han visste at det kom fra den retningen. Så her går han med armene strukket ut, de fillete ermene hengende ned, tårer rennende fra stedet hvor øynene skulle være, løpende mot Ham. Han visste ikke hvordan han skulle finne Ham, men Guds Ord sa: "Kom," og han gikk.
Det er hovedsaken. Uansett hvor syk du er, hvor dårlig du er, hvor mange prøvelser du har hatt. Når Han sa: "Hver den som vil, la ham komme," mente Han deg. Det er riktig.
"Hvordan skal jeg komme dit, Bror Branham? Legen sa at jeg ikke kan bli frisk."
Kast det til side. La oss gå. Vi har Hans oppmerksomhet i kveld. Han passerer denne veien. Han har vært her hver kveld denne uka. Han er her nå. Han vil være hvor som helst hvor to eller tre er samlet i Hans navn: "Jeg vil være midt iblant dem. Alt de blir enige om å be om, skal de få." Vi har Hans oppmerksomhet nå. Han beveger seg gjennom bygningen.
Nå, løft dine troens hender til Ham og si, "Herre, at jeg må få synet mitt."
Han sa,

text:
"What would you have…?"
Now, Jesus didn't say, "Now, will you sign a pledge card to go to church six months out of the year? What church will you join if I heal you?" That wasn't even in the question. The main thing was: "Get the man his desire."
That's the thing tonight. No matter whether you're Methodist, Baptist, Presbyterian, Roman Catholic, or whatever you are, if you ask Jesus Christ for healing tonight, He'll do it. That's all. He's promised it in His Word, and He's obligated to His Word to do it. That's right. He'll take care of the rest of it, if you'll just obey Him there. Just believe His Word.
There he was, "That I might receive my sight, Lord."
And He looked around to him and said, "Thy faith has saved thee." He never said, "Now, I know that you're going to be well." He just said, "Your faith has saved you." He knew that if that man respected the gift of God in that measure, God would be obligated to reward him of his faith. There you are. He come with a full heart. Jesus knowed what he was coming for. Jesus knowed what he was looking for. He knew the depths of his faith. And He said, "Thy faith has saved thee."
The man turned around and said, "I'm going to get it. It'll be mine." Walked around, said, "I begin to see daylight already." And away he went. He could see daylight. God had healed him.
text_no:
«Hva vil du at Jeg skal gjøre for deg?»
Jesus sa ikke: «Vil du signere et løfte om å gå i menigheten seks måneder i året? Hvilken menighet vil du bli medlem av hvis Jeg helbreder deg?» Det var ikke engang et tema. Hovedsaken var: «Gi mannen det han ønsker.»
Det er det samme i kveld. Uansett om du er metodist, baptist, presbyterianer, katolikk eller noe annet, hvis du spør Jesus Kristus om helbredelse i kveld, vil Han gjøre det. Det er alt. Han har lovet det i sitt Ord, og Han er forpliktet til sitt Ord. Det stemmer. Han tar seg av resten hvis du bare adlyder Ham. Bare tro Hans Ord.
Der var han: «At jeg må få synet mitt, Herre.»
Jesus så på ham og sa: «Din tro har frelst deg.» Han sa aldri: «Jeg vet at du kommer til å bli bra.» Han sa bare: «Din tro har frelst deg.» Jesus visste at hvis denne mannen respekterte Guds gave så mye, ville Gud være forpliktet til å belønne ham for hans tro. Der har du det. Mannen kom med et fullt hjerte. Jesus visste hva han kom for. Jesus visste hva han så etter. Han kjente dybden av hans tro. Og Han sa: «Din tro har frelst deg.»
Mannen snudde seg og sa: «Jeg skal få det. Det blir mitt.» Han gikk rundt og sa: «Jeg begynner allerede å se dagslys.» Og bort gikk han. Han kunne se dagslys. Gud hadde helbredet ham.

text:
I read a little story here, not long ago; maybe it's mythical, maybe it's not. I don't know. They say that Bartimaeus was born blind. Said he used to have two little turtledoves that they would turn over one another and do little enchantments that'd attract the tourists' attention that was coming by. In order … so many beggars, if he didn't have something to attract their attention, he'd never get a coin. So they would do little tumbles over each other.
Said he had a little girl he had never seen. And the girl got sick one night, and the doctor could do nothing for her. And he went out and prayed and said, "Lord, if You will heal my little girl, tomorrow I'll offer these turtledoves as a sacrifice."
Well, the next day the little girl was better. Immediately, blind Bartimaeus goes up to the … or, the sacrifice to the temple, and gives the sacrifice over. He didn't have no turtledoves now.
text_no:
Jeg leste en liten historie nylig; kanskje er den mytisk, kanskje ikke. Jeg vet ikke. De sier at Bartimeus ble født blind. Det sies at han pleide å ha to små turtelduer som snurret rundt hverandre og utførte små kunster for å tiltrekke seg turistenes oppmerksomhet. Med så mange tiggere, måtte han ha noe som fanget oppmerksomheten for å få en mynt. Derfor fikk duene gjøre små triks.
Det sies at han hadde en liten jente han aldri hadde sett. En natt ble jenta syk, og legen kunne ikke gjøre noe for henne. Bartimeus gikk ut og ba: "Herre, hvis Du helbreder min lille jente, vil jeg i morgen ofre disse turtelduene som en takk."
Neste dag var jenta bedre. Umiddelbart gikk den blinde Bartimeus til tempelet og ofret duene. Nå hadde han ingen turtelduer lenger.

text:
And then one night, they said his wife got sick, sometime later. And nowadays, a blind man is led by a dog. Them days, a blind person was led by a lamb. A dog was a cursed thing amongst the Jews. And they… So he had a lamb. And so, the lamb led him.
And so the next morning, he was going up to the sacrifice to offer his lamb because his wife got well … or, was better the next morning. And it said … Caiaphas, the great high priest said, "Where goest thou, blind Bartimaeus?"
He said, "Oh, priest, I am going up to offer my lamb for a sacrifice." He told him the story that his wife was sick and he promised God that he would give the sacrifice of the lamb if He would heal his wife. And God had healed his wife, so he was going to make his word good.
"Why," he said, "blind Bartimaeus, I'll give you the price of a lamb, and you buy it at the stalls."
And he said, "No, I never offered God a lamb, I offered Him this lamb. This was the lamb that I offered." That's it.
You try to say, "Well, God was good to me; I'll to this."
Now, God wants you. You're the lamb that God's after, see.
"I'll send my children to Sunday School."
That's wrong. You must take them to Sunday School, see. You must go, too. God wants you. Now, be an example.
text_no:
En natt ble det sagt at kona hans ble syk. I dag blir en blind mann ledet av en hund, men den gangen ble en blind person ledet av et lam. En hund ble sett på som urent blant jødene. Så han hadde et lam som ledet ham.
Neste morgen gikk han opp for å ofre sitt lam fordi kona hans hadde blitt bedre. Caiaphas, den store ypperstepresten, sa: "Hvor går du, blinde Bartimeus?"
Han svarte: "Å, prest, jeg går for å ofre mitt lam som et offer." Han fortalte at kona hans hadde vært syk, og at han hadde lovet Gud å ofre lammet hvis Gud helbredet henne. Gud hadde helbredet henne, så nå ville han holde sitt løfte.
Caiaphas sa: "Blinde Bartimeus, jeg gir deg prisen for et lam, og du kan kjøpe ett på markedet."
Men Bartimeus svarte: "Nei, jeg tilbød aldri Gud et annet lam, jeg tilbød Ham dette lammet. Dette er lammet jeg tilbød." Det er poenget.
Noen sier: "Gud var god mot meg; jeg skal gjøre dette." Men Gud vil ha deg. Du er lammet som Gud søker.
Å si: "Jeg vil sende barna mine til søndagsskolen" er feil. Du må ta dem med til søndagsskolen. Du må gå selv også. Gud vil ha deg. Vær et eksempel.

text:
Now, notice. And then the high priest said, "Blind Bartimaeus, you cannot offer this lamb. Why, blind Bartimaeus, that lamb is your eyes. Why, you can't offer that lamb, it's your eyes." And said…
He said, "Oh high priest, God will provide a lamb for blind Bartimaeus' eyes. I promised Him this lamb and I must offer it."
Well, on that cold afternoon, when that noise came down the street; God had provided a Lamb for blind Bartimaeus' eye. He give him back his eyes.
That same Lamb is provided tonight, here in this church for your sickness, for your troubles, for your heartache, for your diseases. For whatever you have wrong, God has a provided Lamb. That's the Lamb of God that gave His life at Calvary for every one of us. For your sickness and your sins, the Lamb is provided for you tonight.
While we're on this well-beaten Ecclesiastical road, where many are passing by, saying, "The days of miracles is past.", can't you hear that little noise somewhere down in your heart: "He rose from the dead. He liveth forevermore." and "I'll be with you, even in you, to the end of the world."?
May He be so real to you tonight, you believers, until you can't help from believing. And may He come and do something just a little different tonight, and make it so real to you, till you go away saying, "Yes, I believe."
text_no:
Legg merke til dette. Overpresten sa: "Blinde Bartimeus, du kan ikke ofre dette lammet. Blinde Bartimeus, det lammet er dine øyne. Du kan ikke ofre det lammet, det er dine øyne." Og han sa...
Han svarte: "Å, overprest, Gud vil skaffe et lam for blinde Bartimeus' øyne. Jeg lovet Ham dette lammet, og jeg må ofre det."
På den kalde ettermiddagen, da den lyden kom nedover gaten, hadde Gud skaffet et Lam for blinde Bartimeus' øyne. Han ga ham synet tilbake.
Det samme Lammet er skaffet i kveld, her i denne menigheten, for din sykdom, dine problemer, dine hjertesorger og dine plager. Uansett hva som plager deg, har Gud skaffet et Lam. Det er Guds Lam som ga Sitt liv på Golgata for hver og en av oss. For dine sykdommer og dine synder er Lammet skaffet til deg i kveld.
Mens vi går på denne velkjente kirkelige veien, hvor mange passerer forbi og sier: "Miraklenes tid er forbi," kan du ikke høre en liten lyd et sted i ditt hjerte: "Han stod opp fra de døde. Han lever for evig." og "Jeg vil være med dere, til og med i dere, helt til verdens ende."
Måtte Han være så virkelig for deg i kveld, dere troende, at dere ikke kan annet enn å tro. Og måtte Han komme og gjøre noe bare litt annerledes i kveld, og gjøre det så virkelig for dere, at dere går derfra og sier: "Ja, jeg tror."

text:
My ministry is not to minister to individuals. The thing of it is, is to come together, sit in heavenly places. Then when you see God's Spirit moving, and you're sure that it's God's Spirit moving, then you just accept God where you are. Accept Jesus, and healing will take care for its own, then. Shall we pray?
Lord, in the name of Jesus the Son of God, that Lamb provided from the foundation of the world, that was made sin from the foundation, that was wounded back there from the foundation for our healing. I pray that people here tonight in Louisville, Kentucky, in this little church tonight, will close the doors of their hearts from all unbelief and refuse to accept it. And may they, tonight, just look to Calvary and say, "Lord, I have known in Thy Word that You said 'The things that I do shall you also.' And, 'I'll be with you, even in you to the end of the world.' And, 'A little while and the world seeth Me no more (the unbeliever) but yet ye shall see Me, for I'll be with you, even in you, to the end of the world.'"
Come, Lord Jesus. Manifest Your love, and compassion, and Yourself to this audience of people tonight, by taking away their iniquity, and healing their diseases. In Jesus Christ's name, the Son of God, I ask it. Amen.
text_no:
Min tjeneste er ikke å betjene enkeltpersoner. Hensikten er å komme sammen og sitte i himmelske steder. Når du ser Guds Ånd bevege seg, og du er sikker på at det er Guds Ånd, da aksepterer du bare Gud der du er. Aksepter Jesus, og helbredelsen vil skje av seg selv. La oss be.
Herre, i Jesu, Guds Sønns navn, det Lammet som ble gitt fra verdens grunnleggelse, som ble gjort til synd fra begynnelsen og ble såret for vår helbredelse. Jeg ber om at menneskene her i kveld i Louisville, Kentucky, i denne lille menigheten, lukker hjertedørene for all vantro og nekter å akseptere den. Må de i kveld bare se til Golgata og si: "Herre, jeg har visst fra Ditt Ord at Du sa 'De ting Jeg gjør, skal dere også gjøre.' Og, 'Jeg vil være med dere, ja, i dere, til verdens ende.' Og, 'En liten stund og verden ser Meg ikke mer (den vantro), men dere skal se Meg, for Jeg vil være med dere, ja, i dere, til verdens ende.'"
Kom, Herre Jesus. Vis Din kjærlighet, medfølelse og Deg Selv for denne forsamlingen i kveld ved å ta bort deres synd og helbrede deres sykdommer. I Jesu Kristi navn, Guds Sønn, ber jeg. Amen.

text:
All right. I believe we was in "Q" last night. Is that right? giving prayer card "Q." Well, let's take 85 to 100, if that number hasn't already been called.
[Break in tape.]
… do what He could only know as the Father showed Him. Is that right? Now how many believers in here know that that's the truth, that Jesus did know nothing, or could do nothing, unless first the Father showed Him by vision? Let's see your hands. That's exactly. He said that. We're aware of it. Now then the Father dealt with Him. He said, "I can do nothing … I do nothing in Myself, but what I see the Father doing, that I'll do."
Then He walked down amongst people who were sick, dying; walked by the dead, everything else, but He didn't… He raised some of them. He healed some of them. But He only did it at the command of Jehovah God, His Father. Is that right. And Jehovah God, His Father, was in Christ reconciling the world to Himself. So Christ was Jehovah God. That's right. He was either God or the biggest deceiver the world's ever had.
If He was just a man, and a different person from God, then you're in sin. His blood was no more than mine or yours. He was either God, and that was Immanuel's blood, or you're still in sin.
text_no:
Alright. Jeg tror vi var på "Q" i går kveld. Stemmer det? Vi delte ut bønnekort "Q." La oss ta nummer 85 til 100, hvis de nummerne ikke allerede har blitt ropt opp. [Tomt.område.på.lydbånd.]
… gjøre bare det Han kunne vite som Faderen viste Ham. Stemmer det? Hvor mange troende her inne vet at det er sannheten, at Jesus ikke visste eller kunne gjøre noe, med mindre Faderen først viste Ham det gjennom en visjon? La oss se hendene deres. Akkurat. Han sa det. Vi er klar over det.
Så samarbeidet Faderen med Ham. Han sa: "Jeg kan ikke gjøre noe … Jeg gjør ingenting av Meg Selv, bare det Jeg ser Faderen gjøre, det gjør Jeg." Deretter gikk Han blant syke og døende mennesker, gikk forbi de døde og alt annet, men Han… Han oppreiste noen av dem. Han helbredet noen av dem. Men Han gjorde det bare etter Jehova Guds, Sin Faders, befaling. Stemmer det? 
Og Jehova Gud, Hans Far, var i Kristus og forsonte verden med Seg Selv. Så Kristus var Jehova Gud. Det er riktig. Han var enten Gud eller verdens største bedrager.
Hvis Han bare var et menneske, og en annen person enn Gud, da er du i synd. Hans blod var ikke mer verdt enn mitt eller ditt. Han var enten Gud, og det var Immanuels blod, eller så er du fortsatt i synd.

text:
Today when they try to break up God and make Him something that He isn't, I tell you, that's one of the biggest curses on the Christian church today.
They say, "Oh, He was a good man. Yes, He was a teacher. He was a philosopher. Oh, He was a prophet. What He said come to pass."
He was more than a teacher, yet He was a teacher. He was more than a prophet, yet He was a prophet. He was God. God was in Christ. He was the Immanuel Jehovah anointed, walking here on earth, living among men.
First Timothy 3:16: "Without controversy, great is the mystery of godliness, for God was manifested in the flesh, seen of angels, preached on the world, believed on in the world, received up into glory."---God Himself.
So now, Jesus was God's manifestation of His own being on earth, unfolding Himself into a human being to take away sin and make a way for sickness to be healed.
And if there is no divine healing, there is no resurrection of the dead. It's the earnest of your resurrection. Amen.
I quit preaching quick tonight, you noticed it: The Angel of the Lord is here. That's right.
Now in the name of Jesus Christ, the Son of God, I take every spirit in here under my control for the glory of God. And may the Lord Jesus Christ, who is my full Guide, that my life is now surrendered to Him as an empty channel that He speaks through, may He have control in my life, that His Spirit may control everything that's in here. And cast out every evil spirit, heal every sick person that's in the building. Believe me as God's servant, for I speak in the name of Jesus Christ. Amen.
text_no:
I dag, når de prøver å splitte opp Gud og gjøre Ham til noe Han ikke er, forteller jeg deg at det er en av de største forbannelsene på den kristne menigheten i dag. De sier: "Å, Han var en god mann. Ja, Han var en lærer. Han var en filosof. Å, Han var en profet. Det Han sa skjedde."
Han var mer enn en lærer, selv om Han var en lærer. Han var mer enn en profet, selv om Han var en profet. Han var Gud. Gud var i Kristus. Han var Immanuel, Jehova salvet, som gikk her på jorden og levde blant menneskene.
Første Timoteus 3:16 sier: "Uten motsigelse, stor er gudsfryktens mysterium, for Gud ble åpenbart i kjød, sett av engler, forkynt blant folkeslag, trodd på i verden, opptatt i herlighet." - Gud selv.
Så Jesus var Guds manifestasjon av Hans eget vesen på jorden, som åpenbarte Seg som et menneske for å ta bort synden og gjøre vei for helbredelse fra sykdom.
Og hvis det ikke finnes noen guddommelig helbredelse, finnes det ingen oppstandelse fra de døde. Det er pantet på din oppstandelse. Amen.
Jeg avsluttet forkynnelsen raskt i kveld, som du la merke til: Herrens Engel er her. Det stemmer.
Nå, i Jesu Kristi, Guds Sønns, navn tar jeg hver ånd her under min kontroll for Guds ære. Må Herren Jesus Kristus, som er min fulle Veileder, og som jeg nå har overgitt mitt liv til som en tom kanal Han taler gjennom, ha kontroll i mitt liv, slik at Hans Ånd kan kontrollere alt som er her. Og kaste ut hver ond ånd, helbrede hver syk person i bygningen. Tro meg som Guds tjener, for jeg taler i Jesu Kristi navn. Amen.

text:
Now, Father, to these handkerchiefs, You know the people. You know what they mean. And I pray that You'll heal every one that these handkerchiefs represent, as I lay my hands upon them. They're not mine now at this time; they're given to You; they're Yours. May Your Holy Spirit charge every handkerchief with a blessing. Grant it, Lord. May every sick person be healed, and God glorified.
One place in the Scripture where God looked down through angry eyes through the pillar of fire, and the Red Sea got scared and moved back. And Israel went over into the land of promise. May sickness tonight that's cut these people off from health, may God look back through that pillar of fire tonight, that every one of these handkerchiefs, and when they're laid upon the sick bodies, may the enemy move back, and the people cross to the promise, of God's promise of good health. For I ask it in Jesus Christ's name. Amen.
text_no:
Nå, Far, til disse lommetørklene, Du kjenner folket. Du vet hva de mener. Og jeg ber om at Du skal helbrede hver eneste en som disse lommetørklene representerer, idet jeg legger mine hender på dem. De er ikke mine nå; de er gitt til Deg; de er Dine. Må Din Hellige Ånd fylle hvert lommetørkle med en velsignelse. Innvilg det, Herre. Må enhver syk person bli helbredet, og Gud bli æret.
I Skriften er det et sted hvor Gud så gjennom den flammesøylen med vrede, og Rødehavet ble skremt og trakk seg tilbake. Og Israel gikk over til løftets land. Må sykdom i kveld som har skilt disse menneskene fra helsen, må Gud se gjennom den flammesøylen i kveld, at hvert av disse lommetørklene, og når de blir lagt på de syke kroppene, må fienden trekke seg tilbake, og folket krysse over til løftet, Guds løfte om god helse. For jeg ber om dette i Jesus Kristus sitt navn. Amen.

text:
All right. Bring your patient.
Now, would everyone reverent just as… Just a moment. This man standing here, a deaf spirit's on him. Bow your heads so we can get that to leave.
O Lord, kind Heavenly Father, feeling this enemy now, as it shakes---would try to hold the man, but… He may be so deaf that he can't even hear your Word; I don't know, Thou does. And now, Lord, if that be the case, let Thy servant's faith mount, Lord, highly. Move down into his body, knowing this: that Thou has promised by laying on of hands, casting out spirits, and here's a spirit of deafness. The man can't hear, perhaps; but Thou, Lord, can give him his hearing.
And now with faith, believing, I come to challenge this spirit that's on him. And in the name of Jesus Christ, who I represent… Calvary, where He won the victory over you, Satan, and all your kind, and your sickness. For He triumphed! And He sent His servants that they might minister His blessings to the people. Come out of the man! In the name Jesus Christ, I charge thee to do this.
[Brother Branham speaks aside.] Do you hear me? How deaf was you? [The man speaks to Bro. Branham.] Thirty years. You may raise your head. Hear me all right? [The man says, "Praise the Lord."] Amen. Say, "I love the Lord." [The man says, "I love the Lord."] What I say low, you say real loud. Praise the Lord. ["Praise the Lord!"] I love Jesus. ["I love Jesus!"] Now, watch. I'm whispering. This microphone's alive. Glory to God. ["Glory to God!"] Now, hold this ear. Praise the Lord! ["Glory to God!"] Glory to Jesus. ["Glory to Jesus!"]
The man is well. Sir, your faith has made you whole. Now, God has blessed you; you serve Him. Yes, I see there's something else wrong with you, also. You have a rupture, don't you? [The man says, "That's right."] That's right? ["That's right."] Do you believe He's going to make that well? ["I do."] God bless you. If He can give you your hearing, He can do that, can't He? You're standing close, and I have a contact with your spirit. You're aware that something's going on, isn't it? Yes, sir. A strange feeling you have never felt this. Isn't that right? Just raise your hand high, see. You're so close now, in anointing of God, you're…
Say, don't you do some kind of work around a jail, or jail services, isn't that right? In a jail? ["About sixteen years."] Yes, sir. I see you do. Yes, sir. And isn't your name Kopp, or something like K-O-double-P. That's is right. Go home now, you're well. God has made you well. God bless you. Go and just be happy and rejoice. Believe.
text_no:
All right. Bring din pasient.
Kan alle være ærbødige et øyeblikk? Denne mannen som står her, har en døv ånd på seg. Bøy hodene deres så vi kan få denne ånden til å forlate ham.
O Herre, nådige Himmelske Far, Jeg føler denne fienden som skjelver her nå---prøver å holde mannen, men… Han kan være så døv at han ikke engang kan høre Ditt Ord; jeg vet ikke, men Det gjør Du.
